sources.full_name: Basel Committee on Banking Supervision standards and guidance
sources.coverage_note: Basel III/IV framework: capital, liquidity, leverage, large exposures, output floor, CVA, operational risk, market risk, credit risk standardised and IRB approaches.

provisions.title: Minimum Capital Requirements
provisions.text: Banks must maintain a minimum total capital ratio of 8% of risk-weighted assets. The capital ratio is calculated as the sum of Tier 1 capital and Tier 2 capital divided by total risk-weighted assets. Tier 1 capital must constitute at least 6% of risk-weighted assets, of which Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) must be at least 4.5%.

provisions.title: Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) Capital
provisions.text: CET1 capital consists of the sum of: common shares issued by the bank that meet the criteria for classification as common shares for regulatory purposes; stock surplus (share premium) resulting from the issue of instruments included in CET1 capital; retained earnings; accumulated other comprehensive income and other disclosed reserves; common shares issued by consolidated subsidiaries of the bank and held by third parties that meet the criteria for inclusion in CET1 capital; and regulatory adjustments applied in the calculation of CET1.

provisions.title: Additional Tier 1 (AT1) Capital
provisions.text: Additional Tier 1 capital instruments must meet specified criteria including: being subordinated to depositors, general creditors and subordinated debt of the bank; having no maturity and no incentive to redeem; being callable at the initiative of the issuer only after a minimum of five years with supervisory approval; and the bank doing nothing to create an expectation that the call will be exercised. AT1 instruments must have a loss absorption mechanism that is triggered at the point of non-viability or when CET1 ratio falls below 5.125%.

provisions.title: Tier 2 Capital
provisions.text: Tier 2 capital instruments must: be subordinated to depositors and general creditors of the bank; have an original maturity of at least five years; not contain incentives to redeem prior to maturity; and be callable at the initiative of the issuer only after five years with supervisory approval. During the final five years to maturity, eligible Tier 2 instruments are amortised on a straight-line basis.

provisions.title: Capital Conservation Buffer
provisions.text: Banks are required to maintain a capital conservation buffer of 2.5% of risk-weighted assets, comprised of CET1 capital. The buffer is designed to ensure banks build up capital outside periods of stress that can be drawn down when losses occur. When a bank's CET1 ratio falls within the buffer range, it is subject to increasing restrictions on discretionary distributions (dividends, share buybacks, bonus payments).

provisions.title: Countercyclical Capital Buffer
provisions.text: The countercyclical buffer ranges from 0% to 2.5% of risk-weighted assets, comprised of CET1 capital. It aims to ensure the banking system has a buffer of capital to protect it against future potential losses when excess aggregate credit growth is associated with a build-up of systemic risk. National authorities set this buffer based on credit-to-GDP gaps and other indicators. Banks exposed to multiple jurisdictions calculate a weighted average based on geographic distribution of credit exposures.

provisions.title: G-SIB Capital Surcharge
provisions.text: Global systemically important banks (G-SIBs) are subject to additional CET1 capital surcharges ranging from 1% to 3.5% of risk-weighted assets, depending on systemic importance score. G-SIBs are identified using a methodology based on five categories: cross-jurisdictional activity, size, interconnectedness, substitutability/financial institution infrastructure, and complexity. The surcharge is phased in with equal annual increments and becomes fully effective in January 2019.

provisions.title: D-SIB Framework
provisions.text: Domestic systemically important banks (D-SIBs) are identified by national authorities based on criteria including size, interconnectedness, substitutability and complexity within the domestic context. National authorities may apply higher loss absorbency (HLA) requirements to D-SIBs. The HLA requirement for D-SIBs should be met with CET1 capital. National authorities determine the level, which should be commensurate with the degree of systemic importance.

provisions.title: Regulatory Deductions from CET1
provisions.text: Key deductions from CET1 include: goodwill and other intangible assets; deferred tax assets that rely on future profitability; cash flow hedge reserves; shortfall of provisions to expected losses (IRB banks); gain on sale related to securitisation transactions; defined benefit pension fund net assets; investments in own shares; reciprocal cross-holdings in banking, financial and insurance entities; and investments in capital of banking, financial and insurance entities that are outside the scope of regulatory consolidation where the bank does not own more than 10% of the issued common share capital.

provisions.title: Minimum Capital Stack Summary
provisions.text: The combined minimum capital requirements under Basel III are: CET1 minimum 4.5% + capital conservation buffer 2.5% = effective CET1 minimum 7.0%; total Tier 1 minimum 6.0% + buffer = 8.5%; total capital minimum 8.0% + buffer = 10.5%. For G-SIBs, additional surcharges of 1-3.5% apply. The countercyclical buffer adds 0-2.5% when activated. National authorities may impose additional Pillar 2 requirements.

provisions.title: Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR)
provisions.text: Banks must maintain an adequate level of unencumbered high-quality liquid assets (HQLA) that can be converted into cash at little or no loss of value in private markets to meet their liquidity needs for a 30 calendar day liquidity stress scenario. The Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) must be at least 100%. LCR = Stock of HQLA / Total net cash outflows over the next 30 calendar days >= 100%.

provisions.title: High-Quality Liquid Assets (HQLA) Definition
provisions.text: HQLA are divided into Level 1 and Level 2 assets. Level 1 assets (no haircut) include: cash, central bank reserves, sovereign securities rated AA- or higher, and certain public sector entity securities. Level 2A assets (15% haircut) include: sovereign securities rated A+ to BBB-, covered bonds rated AA- or higher, and corporate bonds rated AA- or higher. Level 2B assets (25-50% haircut) include: RMBS rated AA or higher, corporate bonds rated A+ to BBB-, and certain equity shares. Level 2 assets may not exceed 40% of the total HQLA stock, of which Level 2B may not exceed 15%.

provisions.title: Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR)
provisions.text: Banks must maintain a stable funding profile in relation to the composition of their assets and off-balance sheet activities. The NSFR requires that available stable funding (ASF) exceeds required stable funding (RSF) at all times: NSFR = Available amount of stable funding / Required amount of stable funding >= 100%. ASF categories include capital, preferred stock with maturity >= 1 year, liabilities with effective maturities >= 1 year, and stable demand deposits. RSF factors range from 0% (cash) to 100% (assets encumbered for >= 1 year).

provisions.title: Liquidity Monitoring Tools
provisions.text: In addition to the LCR and NSFR, supervisors should use additional monitoring metrics including: contractual maturity mismatch (mapping of contractual cash flows); concentration of funding (significant counterparty and instrument concentrations); available unencumbered assets (HQLA not pledged as collateral); LCR by significant currency; and market-related monitoring tools (market-wide and bank-specific data on asset prices, CDS spreads, and equity prices).

provisions.title: Intraday Liquidity Management
provisions.text: Banks should actively manage their intraday liquidity positions and risks to meet payment and settlement obligations on a timely basis under both normal and stressed conditions. A bank should have the capacity to measure expected daily gross liquidity inflows and outflows, anticipate the intraday timing of these flows where possible, and forecast the range of net funding shortfalls that might arise at different points during the day.

provisions.title: Leverage Ratio Requirement
provisions.text: Banks must maintain a minimum leverage ratio of 3%. The leverage ratio is calculated as Tier 1 capital divided by total exposure measure. The exposure measure consists of: on-balance sheet exposures (excluding derivatives and SFTs); derivative exposures (using SA-CCR or simplified SA-CCR); securities financing transaction (SFT) exposures; and off-balance sheet items (using credit conversion factors). The leverage ratio acts as a non-risk-based backstop to the risk-weighted capital requirements.

provisions.title: G-SIB Leverage Ratio Buffer
provisions.text: G-SIBs are subject to an additional leverage ratio buffer equal to 50% of their risk-weighted G-SIB surcharge. For example, a G-SIB with a 2% risk-weighted surcharge must meet a leverage ratio buffer of 1%, for a total leverage ratio requirement of 4%. The buffer must be met with Tier 1 capital. When the buffer is breached, the bank is subject to minimum capital conservation requirements (restrictions on distributions).

provisions.title: Large Exposures Framework
provisions.text: A bank's exposure to a single counterparty or group of connected counterparties must not exceed 25% of the bank's Tier 1 capital at all times. For G-SIBs, the limit for exposures to other G-SIBs is 15% of Tier 1 capital. The large exposure limit applies to the sum of all exposure values of a bank to a single counterparty or to a group of connected counterparties, after applying credit risk mitigation techniques and exemptions.

provisions.title: Connected Counterparties
provisions.text: Banks must identify groups of connected counterparties by assessing control relationships (one counterparty controls the other) and economic interdependence (if one counterparty experiences financial problems, the other is likely to encounter repayment difficulties). Where both criteria exist, the counterparties must be treated as a single counterparty. Banks must have policies and procedures to identify such connections and aggregate exposures accordingly.

provisions.title: Revised Standardised Approach for Credit Risk
provisions.text: Under the Basel IV revised standardised approach, exposures to banks are risk-weighted based on either the External Credit Risk Assessment Approach (ECRA) or the Standardised Credit Risk Assessment Approach (SCRA). Under SCRA, banks are classified into grades A, B, or C based on published minimum regulatory requirements (CET1 >= 7% and leverage ratio >= 3% for Grade A). Risk weights range from 20% (Grade A, short-term) to 150% (Grade C).

provisions.title: Corporate Exposures Risk Weights
provisions.text: Under the revised standardised approach, corporate exposures are risk-weighted according to external credit ratings where available: AAA to AA- = 20%, A+ to A- = 50%, BBB+ to BBB- = 75%, BB+ to BB- = 100%, below BB- = 150%, unrated = 100%. Specialised lending exposures (project finance, object finance, commodities finance) receive specific risk weights: 130% for project finance (pre-operational) and 100% (operational); 100% for object finance; 100% for commodities finance.

provisions.title: Real Estate Exposures
provisions.text: Residential real estate exposures are risk-weighted based on loan-to-value (LTV) ratio when the repayment is not materially dependent on cash flows generated by the property: LTV <= 50% = 20%, 50% < LTV <= 60% = 25%, 60% < LTV <= 80% = 30%, 80% < LTV <= 90% = 40%, 90% < LTV <= 100% = 50%, LTV > 100% = 70%. For income-producing residential real estate where repayment depends on cash flows from the property, higher risk weights apply: LTV <= 60% = 30%, 60% < LTV <= 80% = 35%, LTV > 80% = 70%.

provisions.title: Sovereign Exposures Risk Weights
provisions.text: Exposures to sovereigns and their central banks are risk-weighted based on external credit ratings: AAA to AA- = 0%, A+ to A- = 20%, BBB+ to BBB- = 50%, BB+ to B- = 100%, below B- = 150%, unrated = 100%. National authorities may apply a 0% risk weight to exposures to their own sovereign (or central bank) denominated and funded in the domestic currency of that sovereign.

provisions.title: Internal Ratings-Based Approach
provisions.text: Under the IRB approach, banks use their own estimated risk parameters to calculate capital requirements. The Foundation IRB (F-IRB) approach requires banks to estimate probability of default (PD) while supervisory values are used for loss given default (LGD), exposure at default (EAD), and maturity (M). The Advanced IRB (A-IRB) approach allows banks to estimate all risk parameters. Under Basel IV, the A-IRB approach is removed for exposures to large and mid-sized corporates (consolidated revenue > EUR 500 million) and to banks and other financial institutions; these must use F-IRB or standardised approach.

provisions.title: IRB Input Floors
provisions.text: Basel IV introduces input floors for IRB risk parameters: PD floor of 5 basis points (0.05%) for all asset classes except qualifying revolving retail (3 bps); LGD floor of 25% for unsecured corporate exposures (F-IRB: supervisory LGD of 45%); EAD floor equal to on-balance sheet amount for undrawn commitments. The input floors prevent banks from using unrealistically low risk parameter estimates that would understate capital requirements.

provisions.title: Output Floor
provisions.text: Under Basel IV, banks using internal models-based approaches for any risk category must ensure that their total risk-weighted assets are no lower than 72.5% of the total risk-weighted assets calculated using the standardised approaches. This output floor is phased in from 50% (1 January 2023) to 72.5% (1 January 2028). The output floor is calculated at the consolidated level, covering credit risk, market risk, operational risk, and CVA risk. Banks calculate RWAs under both internal models and standardised approaches, and use the higher of: (a) internal models RWAs, and (b) 72.5% of standardised RWAs.

provisions.title: Output Floor Phase-In Schedule
provisions.text: The output floor is phased in according to the following schedule: 50% from 1 January 2023; 55% from 1 January 2024; 60% from 1 January 2025; 65% from 1 January 2026; 70% from 1 January 2027; 72.5% from 1 January 2028. National supervisors may elect to apply transitional arrangements including a cap on the increase in total RWA that results from the application of the floor.

provisions.title: Fundamental Review of the Trading Book (FRTB)
provisions.text: The revised market risk framework (FRTB) introduces revised standardised and internal models approaches. The revised standardised approach (SA) uses a sensitivities-based method where capital charges are calculated by aggregating risk charges from delta, vega and curvature risk factors across risk classes (GIRR, CSR, equity, commodity, FX). Risk weights and correlations are specified. The default risk charge (DRC) captures jump-to-default risk. The residual risk add-on captures exotic risks not addressed by delta, vega, or curvature.

provisions.title: Internal Models Approach for Market Risk
provisions.text: Banks that receive supervisory approval may use the internal models approach (IMA) for market risk. The IMA calculates capital requirements as the sum of: expected shortfall (ES) at a 97.5% confidence level, calibrated to a period of stress; default risk charge (DRC) based on a two-factor model; and stressed capital add-on (SES). Banks must pass model validation tests including P&L attribution and backtesting at the trading desk level. Desks that fail these tests must revert to the standardised approach.

provisions.title: Trading Book / Banking Book Boundary
provisions.text: Instruments must be assigned to either the trading book or the banking book at inception. Instruments held for any of the following purposes must be in the trading book: short-term resale; profiting from short-term price movements; locking in arbitrage profits; hedging risks arising from instruments in the trading book. Strict limits are placed on switching instruments between books. Any switch requires senior management approval, must be reported to supervisors, and any capital benefit from the switch is disallowed.

provisions.title: CVA Risk Framework
provisions.text: Banks must calculate a capital charge for CVA risk arising from changes in CVA driven by movements in counterparty credit spreads and market risk factors that affect CVA. The revised CVA framework provides two approaches: the basic approach (BA-CVA) which uses a standardised formula based on regulatory risk weights and exposure amounts, and the standardised approach (SA-CVA) which is aligned with the FRTB standardised approach methodology. Banks with aggregate notional of non-centrally cleared derivatives <= EUR 100 billion may use a simplified calculation.

provisions.title: Standardised Measurement Approach for Operational Risk
provisions.text: Basel IV replaces all existing operational risk approaches with a single standardised measurement approach (SMA). The SMA capital requirement is based on two components: the Business Indicator Component (BIC), which is a financial-statement-based proxy for operational risk; and the Internal Loss Multiplier (ILM), which adjusts the BIC based on a bank's historical operational loss experience. The Business Indicator (BI) is calculated from three sub-components: interest, leases and dividend component (ILDC); services component (SC); and financial component (FC).

provisions.title: Operational Risk Loss Data Requirements
provisions.text: Banks subject to the SMA that have a Business Indicator above EUR 1 billion must use at least 10 years of good-quality operational loss data to calculate the Internal Loss Multiplier. Loss data collection must cover all material activities and exposures. Banks must have documented policies and procedures for the identification, collection and treatment of internal loss data. Minimum loss thresholds are EUR 20,000 for loss data collection and EUR 100,000 for ILM calculation purposes.

provisions.title: Securitisation Framework
provisions.text: The revised securitisation framework provides a hierarchy of approaches: SEC-IRBA (for banks using IRB for underlying exposures), SEC-ERBA (using external ratings), and SEC-SA (standardised approach). The framework imposes a risk weight floor of 15% for senior tranches (10% for simple, transparent and standardised (STS) securitisations). Capital requirements for re-securitisations carry a floor of 100%. Originating banks must meet significant risk transfer (SRT) criteria to obtain capital relief, including retention of at least 5% net economic interest.

provisions.title: Standardised Approach for Counterparty Credit Risk (SA-CCR)
provisions.text: SA-CCR replaces both the Current Exposure Method (CEM) and Standardised Method (SM) for measuring counterparty credit risk exposure. Exposure at Default = alpha * (Replacement Cost + Potential Future Exposure), where alpha = 1.4. Replacement cost captures the current exposure. Potential future exposure captures future variability using an aggregation formula across five asset classes (interest rate, FX, credit, equity, commodity) with supervisory-set delta factors, supervisory duration, and supervisory factors.

provisions.title: Pillar 3 Disclosure Requirements
provisions.text: Banks must publish a comprehensive set of disclosures that provide market participants with information on a bank's risk profile, capital adequacy, and risk management practices. Key disclosure areas include: composition of capital, capital requirements, credit risk exposures, market risk, operational risk, leverage ratio, liquidity ratios, remuneration, and counterparty credit risk. Large internationally active banks must disclose quantitative information quarterly and qualitative information annually. The revised Pillar 3 framework introduces standardised disclosure templates.

provisions.title: Key Metrics Disclosure (KM1)
provisions.text: Banks must disclose key regulatory metrics in a standardised template (KM1) including: CET1 capital ratio, Tier 1 capital ratio, total capital ratio, CET1 available after meeting minimum capital requirements, capital conservation buffer, countercyclical buffer, G-SIB/D-SIB buffer, leverage ratio, total HQLA, LCR, NSFR, and total loss-absorbing capacity (TLAC) for G-SIBs. These must be disclosed quarterly.

provisions.title: Board Responsibilities
provisions.text: The board of directors has overall responsibility for the bank, including approving and overseeing management's implementation of the bank's strategic objectives, governance framework and corporate culture. The board should exercise its duty of care and duty of loyalty to the bank under applicable law. The board should establish and be satisfied with the bank's organisational structure, including checks and balances such as separation of the risk management and control functions from the business lines.

provisions.title: Risk Management Function
provisions.text: Banks should have an independent risk management function with sufficient authority, stature, independence, resources and access to the board. The chief risk officer (CRO) should have a direct reporting line to the board or board risk committee and should not be removed without board approval. The risk management function should identify, measure, monitor, control and report on all material risks on a firm-wide basis, including concentration risks.

provisions.title: Compliance Function
provisions.text: Banks should have an independent compliance function that is adequately resourced and empowered. The compliance function should have formal status within the bank, with a head of compliance appointed. The compliance function should identify, assess, monitor and report on the bank's compliance risk. It should advise senior management on compliance laws, rules and standards and keep them informed on developments in the compliance landscape.

provisions.title: Internal Audit Function
provisions.text: The bank should have an internal audit function with sufficient authority, stature, independence, resources and access to the board. The internal audit function should provide independent assurance to the board and senior management on the quality and effectiveness of the bank's internal control, risk management and governance systems and processes. Internal audit should have unfettered access to all records, personnel and physical properties relevant to the performance of its activities.

provisions.title: Remuneration
provisions.text: Banks should implement compensation practices that are consistent with and promote sound risk management. Variable remuneration should be adjusted for all types of risk including future risks (malus/clawback). A substantial portion (40-60%) of variable remuneration should be deferred over a period of at least three years. At least 50% of variable remuneration should be in shares or share-linked instruments. Guaranteed variable remuneration is not consistent with sound risk management.

provisions.title: Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process
provisions.text: Supervisors should regularly assess banks' risk profiles, internal governance, risk management capabilities, and capital and liquidity adequacy. The SREP process covers four key areas: (1) banks should have a process for assessing their overall capital adequacy in relation to their risk profile (ICAAP); (2) supervisors should review and evaluate banks' internal capital adequacy assessments and strategies; (3) supervisors should expect banks to operate above minimum regulatory capital ratios; (4) supervisors should seek to intervene at an early stage to prevent capital from falling below prudent levels.

provisions.title: Interest Rate Risk in the Banking Book
provisions.text: Banks must measure, monitor and control interest rate risk in the banking book (IRRBB) and submit results of internal measurement systems to supervisors. The standardised framework measures EVE (economic value of equity) and NII (net interest income) impacts under six prescribed interest rate shock scenarios. The outlier test identifies banks where a standardised interest rate shock causes a decline in EVE of more than 15% of Tier 1 capital. Supervisors should require additional capital or risk reduction where IRRBB is excessive.

provisions.title: Stress Testing Principles
provisions.text: Banks should have comprehensive stress testing programmes that cover a range of risks and business areas. Stress testing should be used as a risk management tool to inform business decisions, capital planning, and liquidity contingency planning. The board and senior management should be actively engaged in the stress testing programme. Stress tests should cover credit risk, market risk, operational risk, liquidity risk and their interactions. Scenarios should include severe but plausible events, reverse stress tests, and multi-factor scenarios capturing systemic interactions.

provisions.title: Principles for Effective Management of Climate-related Financial Risks
provisions.text: Banks should incorporate climate-related financial risks into their governance, risk management, and disclosure frameworks. The 18 principles cover: corporate governance (board oversight of climate risks); internal control framework (policies and procedures); capital and liquidity adequacy (incorporating climate risks into ICAAP/ILAAP); risk management process (identification, measurement, monitoring of physical and transition risks); management monitoring and reporting; comprehensive management of credit, market, liquidity and operational risks arising from climate change; and scenario analysis.

provisions.title: Sound Management of Risks Related to Money Laundering and Financing of Terrorism
provisions.text: Banks should have adequate policies, procedures and controls to manage and mitigate ML/TF risks. The board of directors is responsible for approving the AML/CFT compliance programme. Banks must conduct institution-wide ML/TF risk assessments, perform CDD on all customers including beneficial owners, monitor transactions for suspicious activity, and file suspicious transaction reports with the FIU. Correspondent banking relationships require enhanced due diligence. Group-wide AML/CFT programmes must be applied consistently across branches and subsidiaries.

provisions.title: Principles for Operational Resilience
provisions.text: Banks should develop and maintain operational resilience capabilities to deliver critical operations through disruption. The 7 principles address: governance (board oversight); operational risk management (identifying critical operations, setting impact tolerances); business continuity planning and testing; dependency mapping (third parties, technology, facilities); ICT and cyber security (protecting information assets); communications and crisis management; and third-party dependency management. Banks must map interconnections and interdependencies that support critical operations.

provisions.title: Prudential Treatment of Cryptoasset Exposures
provisions.text: Banks' cryptoasset exposures are classified into two groups. Group 1 includes tokenised traditional assets and cryptoassets with effective stabilisation mechanisms (stablecoins meeting specific conditions); these receive existing Basel Framework treatment with add-ons. Group 2 includes unbacked cryptoassets and stablecoins that fail to meet Group 1 conditions; these are subject to a 1250% risk weight (under the conservative approach) or a modified version of the market risk framework. Total Group 2 exposures must not exceed 2% of Tier 1 capital (1% threshold for immediate escalation).

definitions.definition: A bank's assets and off-balance-sheet exposures, weighted according to risk. Risk weighting is used to calculate a bank's capital ratios under the Basel framework.
The highest quality component of a bank's capital, consisting of common shares, retained earnings, and other comprehensive income. CET1 must absorb losses on a going-concern basis.
A short-term liquidity measure requiring banks to hold sufficient high-quality liquid assets to cover total net cash outflows over a 30-day stress scenario.
A structural liquidity measure requiring banks to maintain a stable funding profile relative to the composition of their assets and off-balance sheet activities over a one-year horizon.
A mechanism ensuring that banks using internal models-based approaches cannot reduce their total risk-weighted assets below 72.5% of the amount calculated using standardised approaches.
Standardised Approach for measuring Counterparty Credit Risk. Replaces the Current Exposure Method for calculating exposure at default for derivatives transactions.

A bank whose distress or disorderly failure would cause significant disruption to the global financial system and economic activity, identified using a methodology based on size, interconnectedness, substitutability, complexity and cross-jurisdictional activity.
